  1135→
  1136→\noindent
  1137→Three cross-checks tie the census to the Diophantine system:
  1138→\begin{itemize}
  1139→\item \textbf{Neutral composites:} The 12 states at $Q_{\text{em}} = 0$
  1140→match $r^2 + s^2 - 1 = 9 + 4 - 1 = 12 = 4N$.
  1141→\item \textbf{Charge $+1/3$ composites} (one up $\times$ one
  1142→down): $rs = 3 \times 2 = 6$ composites, matching equation
  1143→\eqref{eq:rs} with $rs = 6 = 2N$.  These states distribute as
  1144→3 from $(\bar{\mathbf{3}},\mathbf{2})_{5/6} \subset \mathbf{24}$
  1145→and 3 from $\overline{\mathbf{15}}$.
  1146→\item \textbf{Charge $-2/3$ composites} (symmetric down--down):
  1147→$r(r+1)/2 = 6$ symmetric pairs fill the color sextet
  1148→$(\mathbf{6},\mathbf{1})_{-2/3} \subset \mathbf{15}$, matching
  1149→equation~\eqref{eq:rr} with $r(r+1)/2 = 6 = 2N$.
  1150→\end{itemize}
  1151→
  1152→\subsection{Anomaly cancellation and asymptotic freedom}
  1153→The representation $\mathbf{24} \oplus \mathbf{15} \oplus
  1154→\overline{\mathbf{15}}$ must be anomaly-free if it is to appear
  1155→in a consistent gauge theory.
  1156→
  1157→\medskip
  1158→\noindent\textbf{Proposition 2} (Anomaly cancellation).
  1159→\textit{The cubic $SU(5)$ anomaly of $\mathbf{24} \oplus
  1160→\mathbf{15} \oplus \overline{\mathbf{15}}$ vanishes.}
  1161→
  1162→\medskip
  1163→\noindent\textit{Proof.}
  1164→The anomaly coefficient $A(\mathbf{R})$ for $SU(n)$ satisfies
  1165→$A(\text{adjoint}) = 0$ (the adjoint is real),
  1166→$A(\mathbf{15}) = n + 4 = 9$, and
  1167→$A(\overline{\mathbf{15}}) = -(n+4) = -9$.  Therefore
  1168→\[
  1169→A(\mathbf{24}) + A(\mathbf{15}) + A(\overline{\mathbf{15}})
  1170→= 0 + 9 + (-9) = 0\,.
  1171→\]
  1172→\hfill$\square$
  1173→
  1174→\medskip
  1175→\noindent\textbf{Proposition 3} (Asymptotic freedom).
  1176→\textit{An $SU(5)$ gauge theory with matter in the
  1177→$\mathbf{24} \oplus \mathbf{15} \oplus \overline{\mathbf{15}}$
  1178→is asymptotically free.}
  1179→
  1180→\medskip
  1181→\noindent\textit{Proof.}
  1182→The one-loop beta-function coefficient is
  1183→\[
  1184→b_0 = \frac{11}{3}\,C_2(G) - \frac{2}{3}\,\sum_{\text{Weyl}} T(\mathbf{R})\,,
  1185→\]
  1186→where $C_2(G) = n = 5$ for $SU(5)$, $T(\mathbf{adj}) = n = 5$,
  1187→and $T(\mathbf{15}) = (n+2)/2 = 7/2$.  The Weyl fermion content
  1188→consists of one adjoint and one $\mathbf{15} + \overline{\mathbf{15}}$
  1189→(treating each as a Weyl fermion), giving
  1190→$\sum T = 5 + 7/2 + 7/2 = 12$.  Therefore
  1191→\[
  1192→b_0 = \frac{11}{3} \times 5 - \frac{2}{3} \times 12
  1193→= \frac{55 - 24}{3} = \frac{31}{3} > 0\,.
  1194→\]
  1195→\hfill$\square$
  1196→
  1197→\medskip
  1198→Both properties are nontrivial: anomaly cancellation ensures
  1199→the gauge symmetry is quantum-mechanically consistent, and
  1200→$b_0 > 0$ ensures the theory is well-defined in the ultraviolet.
  1201→Together they confirm that the representation forced by the
  1202→sBootstrap counting is physically viable.
  1203→
  1204→\subsection{Summary of the counting argument}
  1205→The logical chain is:
  1206→\begin{enumerate}
  1207→\item The sBootstrap postulate (composites $=$ scalars of the SSM)
  1208→yields three Diophantine equations in $(N, r, s)$.
  1209→\item The unique positive-integer solution is $(3, 3, 2)$:
  1210→three generations, with the symmetric $\mathbf{15}$ forced over
  1211→the antisymmetric $\overline{\mathbf{10}}$.
  1212→\item The solution organizes into $SU(5)$ representations
  1213→$\mathbf{24} \oplus \mathbf{15} \oplus \overline{\mathbf{15}}$,
  1214→which are anomaly-free and asymptotically free.
  1215→\item Right-handed neutrinos are required: without them, no
  1216→integer solution exists.
  1217→\item The top quark is excluded from the composite sector by
  1218→the counting itself ($s = 2 < N = 3$), matching its
  1219→kinematic decoupling ($m_t \gg \Lambda_{\text{QCD}}$).
  1220→\end{enumerate}
  1221→
  1222→%======================================================================
  1223→\section{The Diquark Sector and SU(5) Flavor}
  1224→\label{sec:diquarks}
  1225→%======================================================================
  1226→
  1227→\subsection{Counting partners}
  1228→
  1229→In the full sBootstrap \cite{Rivero2005}, the scalar partners of 
  1230→the Standard Model fermions include both mesons ($q\bar{q}$) and 
  1231→diquarks ($qq$). For five active flavors, the meson matrix gives 
  1232→$5 \times 5 = 25$ scalars. The diquarks $[q_i q_j]$ form a 
  1233→representation of $SU(5)_f$:
  1234→\begin{equation}
  1235→\mathbf{5} \otimes \mathbf{5} = \overline{\mathbf{10}}_A 
  1236→\oplus \mathbf{15}_S\,.
  1237→\end{equation}
  1238→
  1239→The symmetric $\mathbf{15}_S$ gives 15 diquarks including 
  1240→same-flavor states $[uu]$, $[dd]$, \ldots, while the antisymmetric 
  1241→$\overline{\mathbf{10}}_A$ gives 10 diquarks with $i \neq j$ only.
  1242→
  1243→\subsection{The symmetric representation}
  1244→\label{subsec:sym15}
  1245→
  1246→The sBootstrap SUSY generator acts asymmetrically on the two
  1247→fermion sectors. When it acts on a \emph{lepton}, it produces a
  1248→meson: $\mathcal{Q} \cdot \ell \sim q\bar{q}$. Since the quark and antiquark 
  1249→are distinguishable, the meson lies in the full 
  1250→$\mathbf{5} \otimes \bar{\mathbf{5}} = \mathbf{24} + \mathbf{1}$. 
  1251→There is no Pauli constraint, and the meson spectrum is perfectly 
  1252→reproduced.
  1253→
  1254→When it acts on a \emph{quark}, it produces a diquark:
  1255→$\mathcal{Q} \cdot q_i \sim q_i q_j$. The bosonic SUSY generator applied to 
  1256→a single flavor index $i$ naturally produces the \textbf{symmetric} 
  1257→product---the $\mathbf{15}_S$ representation, not the antisymmetric 
  1258→$\overline{\mathbf{10}}$. This is the representation identified 
  1259→in the $SO(32)$ decomposition \cite{Rivero2005}: the adjoint of 
  1260→$SO(32)$ under $SU(5)_f$ decomposes to include the 
  1261→$\mathbf{15} + \overline{\mathbf{15}}$ (diquarks) alongside the 
  1262→$\mathbf{1} + \mathbf{24}$ (mesons).
  1263→
  1264→The 15 includes 5 same-flavor states ($[uu]$, $[dd]$, $[ss]$, 
  1265→$[cc]$, $[bb]$) absent from the $\overline{\mathbf{10}}$. Among 
  1266→these are exotic $+4/3$-charged diquarks such as $[uu]$. The cost: 
  1267→an additional chiral $+4/3$ quark per generation, as identified 
  1268→in \cite{Rivero2005}. Whether these exotic states appear in nature 
  1269→as resonances, or whether they decouple at some high scale, is an 
  1270→open question.
  1271→
  1272→\subsection{The Pauli theorem for scalar diquarks}
  1273→\label{subsec:pauli}
  1274→
  1275→The requirement of the symmetric $\mathbf{15}$ conflicts with 
  1276→Fermi statistics---not merely for $L=0$ diquarks, but for 
  1277→\emph{all} scalar diquarks in the color $\bar{3}$ channel, 
  1278→regardless of internal orbital structure.
  1279→
  1280→\medskip
  1281→\noindent\textbf{Theorem.}
  1282→\textit{For a spin-0 ($J=0$) diquark in the color $\bar{3}$ 
  1283→(antisymmetric) representation, the flavor wave function is 
  1284→antisymmetric for \textbf{all} values of the orbital angular 
  1285→momentum $L$ and spin $S$.}
  1286→
  1287→\medskip
  1288→\noindent\textit{Proof.}
  1289→$J = 0$ requires $L = S$ (since $|L - S| \leq J \leq L + S$). 
  1290→The exchange symmetry of the spin-orbital wave function is 
  1291→$(-1)^{L+S+1} = (-1)^{2L+1} = -1$ for all $L$. Combined with 
  1292→the color $\bar{3}$ factor $(-1)$, the non-flavor product is 
  1293→$(-1)(-1) = +1$ (symmetric). Total antisymmetry then requires 
  1294→flavor to be antisymmetric: $\overline{\mathbf{10}}$. \hfill$\square$
  1295→
  1296→\medskip
  1297→This result is purely kinematic---independent of dynamics, 
  1298→binding mechanisms, or spatial extent. No wave function, however 
  1299→extended, can produce a symmetric-flavor scalar diquark in color 
  1300→$\bar{3}$ while respecting Fermi statistics. The key cases:
  1301→
  1302→\begin{center}
  1303→\begin{tabular}{ccccc}
  1304→\toprule
  1305→$L$ & $S$ & Spin-orbital $(-1)^{2L+1}$ & $\times$ Color & Flavor \\
  1306→\midrule
  1307→0 & 0 & $-1$ & $\times\,(-1) = +1$ & must be A \\
  1308→1 & 1 & $-1$ & $\times\,(-1) = +1$ & must be A \\
  1309→2 & 2 & $-1$ & $\times\,(-1) = +1$ & must be A \\
  1310→\bottomrule
  1311→\end{tabular}
  1312→\end{center}
  1313→
  1314→The $L=1$, $S=1$ row closes the ``P-wave diquark'' escape route 
  1315→that might otherwise seem available.
  1316→
  1317→The theorem has a sharp consequence: \textbf{the sBootstrap 
  1318→diquark in the $\mathbf{15}$ cannot be a $qq$ composite.} It 
  1319→must be either:
  1320→\begin{enumerate}
  1321→\item An algebraically generated object---the image of the 
  1322→Miyazawa supercharge \cite{Miyazawa, CattoGursey} acting on 
  1323→an antiquark, whose symmetry is determined by the superalgebra 
  1324→rather than by the statistics of constituent quarks; or
  1325→\item A fundamental string-theoretic object, as in the $SO(32)$ 
  1326→decomposition \cite{Rivero2005}, where the $\mathbf{15}$ 
  1327→arises from the adjoint representation of the gauge group and 
  1328→the open-string diquark is not built from point-like quarks.
  1329→\end{enumerate}
  1330→
  1331→The contrast with the meson sector is instructive. When the 
  1332→SUSY generator acts on a lepton, it produces a meson 
  1333→($q\bar{q}$): the quark and antiquark are distinguishable, 
  1334→no Pauli constraint applies, and the meson spectrum is 
  1335→reproduced without tension. When it acts on a quark, it 
  1336→produces a diquark in the ``wrong'' representation---and the 
  1337→theorem shows this cannot be fixed by any internal structure 
  1338→of a $qq$ pair. The asymmetry between the two SUSY directions 
  1339→is absolute: mesons work as composites; diquarks do not. 
  1340→This is an unresolved obstruction: the sBootstrap requires
  1341→the symmetric $\mathbf{15}$, but QCD composite diquarks cannot
  1342→fill it. The proposed resolutions---Miyazawa supercharges and
  1343→SO(32) string embeddings---are directions for future work, not
  1344→established mechanisms. The diquark sector remains the weakest
  1345→point of the program.
  1346→
  1347→%======================================================================
  1348→\section{Two Algebraic Structures}
  1349→\label{sec:two_structures}
  1350→%======================================================================
  1351→
  1352→The sBootstrap particle content and mass spectrum are constrained by
  1353→two \emph{independent} algebraic structures.  Part~A
  1354→(\S\ref{subsec:so32_chain}--\ref{subsec:so32_open}) derives the
  1355→representation content from an $SO(32)$ embedding.  Part~B
  1356→(\S\ref{subsec:casimir_eq}--\ref{subsec:casimir_open}) derives an
  1357→electroweak mass ratio from a Casimir eigenvalue equation.
  1358→No algebraic connection between the two has been found
  1359→(see \S\ref{subsec:two_gap}).
  1360→
  1361→%----------------------------------------------------------------------
  1362→\subsection{Part A: The SO(32) embedding chain}
  1363→\label{subsec:so32_chain}
  1364→%----------------------------------------------------------------------
  1365→
  1366→The $SO(32)$ gauge group of Type~I / heterotic string theory
  1367→admits the maximal-subgroup chain
  1368→\begin{equation}
  1369→\label{eq:so32_chain}
  1370→SO(32) \;\supset\; SU(16) \times U(1)_A
  1371→  \;\supset\; SU(5) \times SU(3) \times U(1)_A \times U(1)_B \,.
  1372→\end{equation}
  1373→The first step is the standard embedding $SO(2n) \supset SU(n)
  1374→\times U(1)$; the second decomposes $SU(16)$ under the regular
  1375→subalgebra $SU(5) \times SU(3) \times U(1)_B$.
  1376→Here $SU(5)$ is the Georgi--Glashow unification group and $SU(3)$
  1377→is a \emph{horizontal} (flavor) symmetry acting on the three
  1378→light generations---it is \textbf{not} $SU(3)_c$.
  1379→
  1380→The fundamental $\mathbf{32}$ of $SO(32)$ decomposes as
  1381→\begin{equation}
  1382→\label{eq:32decomp}
  1383→\mathbf{32} = \underbrace{(\mathbf{5},\mathbf{3})}_{R_1}
  1384→  \;\oplus\; \underbrace{(\bar{\mathbf{5}},\bar{\mathbf{3}})}_{R_2}
  1385→  \;\oplus\; \underbrace{(\mathbf{1},\mathbf{1})}_{R_3}
  1386→  \;\oplus\; \underbrace{(\mathbf{1},\mathbf{1})}_{R_4}\,,
  1387→\end{equation}
  1388→where $\dim = 15 + 15 + 1 + 1 = 32$ and the four summands carry
  1389→distinct $U(1)_A \times U(1)_B$ charges.
  1390→
  1391→\subsection{Decomposition of the adjoint}
  1392→The adjoint of $SO(32)$ is the antisymmetric square of the
  1393→fundamental: $\mathbf{496} = \wedge^2(\mathbf{32})$.  Distributing
  1394→over the four summands in \eqref{eq:32decomp} and using the identity
  1395→\begin{equation}
  1396→\wedge^2(\mathbf{5},\mathbf{3})
  1397→  = \bigl[\wedge^2(\mathbf{5}) \otimes S^2(\mathbf{3})\bigr]
  1398→    \;\oplus\;
  1399→    \bigl[S^2(\mathbf{5}) \otimes \wedge^2(\mathbf{3})\bigr]
  1400→  = (\mathbf{10},\mathbf{6})
  1401→    \;\oplus\;
  1402→    (\mathbf{15},\bar{\mathbf{3}})\,,
  1403→\end{equation}
  1404→one obtains the full decomposition listed in
  1405→Table~\ref{tab:496}.
  1406→
  1407→\begin{table}[ht]
  1408→\centering
  1409→\caption{Decomposition of the $SO(32)$ adjoint $\mathbf{496}$
  1410→under $SU(5) \times SU(3) \times U(1)_A \times U(1)_B$.}
  1411→\label{tab:496}
  1412→\begin{tabular}{lcrrc}
  1413→\toprule
  1414→$SU(5)\!\times\!SU(3)$ rep & Dim & $Q_A$ & $Q_B$ & Origin \\
  1415→\midrule
  1416→\multicolumn{5}{l}{\textit{Adjoint sector
  1417→  ($Q_A = Q_B = 0$, 226 states):}} \\[3pt]
  1418→$(\mathbf{24},\,\mathbf{8})$ & 192 & $0$ & $0$
  1419→  & $R_1 \otimes R_2$ \\
  1420→$(\mathbf{24},\,\mathbf{1})$ & 24  & $0$ & $0$
  1421→  & $R_1 \otimes R_2$ \\
  1422→$(\mathbf{1},\,\mathbf{8})$  & 8   & $0$ & $0$
  1423→  & $R_1 \otimes R_2$ \\
  1424→$(\mathbf{1},\,\mathbf{1})$  & 1   & $0$ & $0$
  1425→  & $R_1 \otimes R_2$ \\
  1426→$(\mathbf{1},\,\mathbf{1})$  & 1   & $0$ & $0$
  1427→  & $R_3 \otimes R_4$ \\
  1428→\midrule
  1429→\multicolumn{5}{l}{\textit{Matter sector
  1430→  ($Q_A,\,Q_B \neq 0$, 270 states):}} \\[3pt]
  1431→$(\mathbf{10},\,\mathbf{6})$  & 60 & $+2$ & $+2$
  1432→  & $\wedge^2(R_1)$ \\
  1433→$(\overline{\mathbf{10}},\,\bar{\mathbf{6}})$ & 60 & $-2$ & $-2$
  1434→  & $\wedge^2(R_2)$ \\
  1435→$(\mathbf{15},\,\bar{\mathbf{3}})$ & 45 & $+2$ & $+2$
  1436→  & $\wedge^2(R_1)$ \\
  1437→$(\overline{\mathbf{15}},\,\mathbf{3})$ & 45 & $-2$ & $-2$
  1438→  & $\wedge^2(R_2)$ \\
  1439→$(\mathbf{5},\,\mathbf{3})_{+}$  & 15 & $+2$ & $-14$
  1440→  & $R_1 \otimes R_3$ \\
  1441→$(\mathbf{5},\,\mathbf{3})_{-}$  & 15 & $0$  & $+16$
  1442→  & $R_1 \otimes R_4$ \\
  1443→$(\bar{\mathbf{5}},\,\bar{\mathbf{3}})_{+}$ & 15 & $-2$ & $+14$
  1444→  & $R_2 \otimes R_3$ \\
  1445→$(\bar{\mathbf{5}},\,\bar{\mathbf{3}})_{-}$ & 15 & $0$  & $-16$
  1446→  & $R_2 \otimes R_4$ \\
  1447→\midrule
  1448→\textbf{Total} & \textbf{496} & & & \\
  1449→\bottomrule
  1450→\end{tabular}
  1451→\end{table}
  1452→
  1453→\subsection{Physical content and the projection problem}
  1454→\label{subsec:so32_open}
  1455→
  1456→Restricting to the adjoint sector $Q_A = Q_B = 0$ and dropping the
  1457→horizontal $SU(3)$ factor, one reads off the $SU(5)$ content:
  1458→\begin{equation}
  1459→\mathbf{24} \;\oplus\; \mathbf{1}\,,
  1460→\end{equation}
  1461→which is precisely the meson/slepton content of the sBootstrap.
  1462→The matter sector at $|Q_A| = 2$ contains
  1463→\begin{equation}
  1464→\mathbf{15} \;\oplus\; \overline{\mathbf{15}}
  1465→  \;\oplus\; \mathbf{10} \;\oplus\; \overline{\mathbf{10}}
  1466→  \;\oplus\; \mathbf{5} \;\oplus\; \bar{\mathbf{5}}\,,
  1467→\end{equation}
  1468→among which the $\mathbf{15} + \overline{\mathbf{15}}$ are
  1469→the symmetric diquarks demanded by the sBootstrap counting
  1470→(\S\ref{subsec:sym15}).
  1471→
  1472→Thus the sBootstrap representations appear in the $SO(32)$
  1473→adjoint: the $\mathbf{24}$ in the neutral sector ($Q_A\!=\!Q_B\!=\!0$)
  1474→and the $\mathbf{15} \oplus \overline{\mathbf{15}}$ in the matter
  1475→sector ($|Q_A|\!=\!2$). However, the remaining $496 - 54 = 442$
  1476→states (the $\mathbf{10}$, $\overline{\mathbf{10}}$,
  1477→$\mathbf{5}$, $\bar{\mathbf{5}}$, the SU(3)-horizontal copies,
  1478→and the neutral singlets) must be projected out or made massive.
  1479→The mechanism for this projection---whether by an orbifold,
  1480→a GSO-type condition, or a flux background---is an open problem.
  1481→
  1482→This embedding was first noted in \cite{Rivero2005}.  Its role
  1483→here is to provide a UV origin for the symmetric $\mathbf{15}$
  1484→that the Pauli theorem (\S\ref{subsec:pauli}) forbids at the
  1485→level of point-particle composites: in the string picture,
  1486→diquarks are open-string states whose symmetry is determined by
  1487→the gauge group, not by Fermi statistics of constituents.
  1488→
  1489→\paragraph{Why $SO(32)$ and not $E_8 \times E_8$.}
  1490→Anomaly cancellation in ten dimensions selects two gauge groups:
  1491→$SO(32)$ and $E_8 \times E_8$. The latter cannot accommodate the
  1492→sBootstrap. Every decomposition of $E_8$ under $SU(5)$ produces
  1493→the antisymmetric $\mathbf{10}$, never the symmetric $\mathbf{15}$:
  1494→the maximal-subgroup chain $E_8 \supset SU(5) \times SU(5)$ gives
  1495→$\mathbf{248} = (\mathbf{24},\mathbf{1}) + (\mathbf{1},\mathbf{24})
  1496→+ (\mathbf{10},\bar{\mathbf{5}}) + (\overline{\mathbf{10}},\mathbf{5})
  1497→+ (\mathbf{5},\mathbf{10}) + (\bar{\mathbf{5}},\overline{\mathbf{10}})$,
  1498→with no $\mathbf{15}$ appearing anywhere.  The obstruction is
  1499→irreducible: it reflects the fact that $E_8$'s simply-laced root

1500→lattice admits only antisymmetric tensor representations of
  1501→$SU(5)$.  By contrast, $SO(32)$'s adjoint $\mathbf{496} =
  1502→\wedge^2(\mathbf{32})$ naturally contains the symmetric
  1503→$\mathbf{15}$ through the identity
  1504→$\wedge^2(\mathbf{5} \otimes \mathbf{3}) \supset
  1505→S^2(\mathbf{5}) \otimes \wedge^2(\mathbf{3})
  1506→= (\mathbf{15}, \bar{\mathbf{3}})$.  The Type~I realization, where
  1507→quark Chan-Paton factors directly generate $SO(32)$ from
  1508→$5\!\times\!3 + 1 = 16$ endpoint labels, is the most natural home.
  1509→
  1510→The $E_8 \times E_8$ heterotic string is connected to Type~I $SO(32)$
  1511→by a chain of dualities (S-duality to heterotic $SO(32)$, then
  1512→T-duality on a non-simply-connected torus).  These dualities may
  1513→allow the sBootstrap content to appear in the $E_8 \times E_8$ frame
  1514→through a non-standard embedding, but the direct decomposition
  1515→route is blocked by the $\mathbf{15}$ vs.\ $\mathbf{10}$
  1516→obstruction.
  1517→
  1518→%----------------------------------------------------------------------
  1519→\subsection{Part B: The Casimir eigenvalue equation}
  1520→\label{subsec:casimir_eq}
  1521→%----------------------------------------------------------------------
  1522→
  1523→Consider the eigenvalue equation
  1524→\begin{equation}
  1525→\label{eq:casimir_quartic}
  1526→x^2 \;+\; C_2\,x \;-\; C_2 = 0\,,
  1527→\qquad C_2 = s(s+1)\,,
  1528→\end{equation}
  1529→where $C_2$ is the quadratic Casimir of $SU(2)$ at spin $s$ and
  1530→$x = M^2/m^2$ for a mass scale $m$ to be determined.  Equivalently,
  1531→$x^2/(1-x) = C_2$: the eigenvalue is a self-consistent solution
  1532→where the ``squared coupling'' $x^2$ equals $C_2$ times the
  1533→complement $(1-x)$.  The solutions are
  1534→\begin{equation}
  1535→\label{eq:casimir_roots}
  1536→x_{\pm}(s) = \frac{-C_2 \pm \sqrt{C_2^2 + 4C_2}}{2}\,.
  1537→\end{equation}
  1538→
  1539→\paragraph{Structural constraints (codimension analysis).}
  1540→The most general two-parameter family of such equations is
  1541→\begin{equation}
  1542→x^2 + (f_0 + f_1 C_2)\,x + (g_0 + g_1 C_2) = 0\,,
  1543→\end{equation}
  1544→with four real coefficients $(f_0, f_1, g_0, g_1)$.  Three
  1545→structural conditions pin the polynomial to \eqref{eq:casimir_quartic}:
  1546→\begin{enumerate}
  1547→\item $f_0 = 0$: no spin-independent mass term;
  1548→\item $g_0 = 0$: a massless eigenstate exists at $s = 0$;
  1549→\item $g_1 = -f_1$: coefficient antisymmetry
  1550→  (the constant and linear terms in $C_2$ are related).
  1551→\end{enumerate}
  1552→Together with the normalization $f_1 = 1$, these exhaust the
  1553→freedom.  A systematic scan of 582 polynomial alternatives
  1554→(quadratics, cubics, quartics with Casimir-type functions and
  1555→rational coefficients) confirms uniqueness: no other
  1556→equation of this class reproduces $\sin^2\!\theta_W$ to
  1557→sub-percent accuracy at $(s_1, s_2) = (1/2, 1)$.
  1558→The ratio derived below is therefore a
  1559→\emph{consequence} of these constraints, not a free parameter.
  1560→
  1561→\subsection{The electroweak ratio}
  1562→Define the ratio
  1563→\begin{equation}
  1564→R \;\equiv\; 1 - \frac{x_+(1/2)}{x_+(1)}\,.
  1565→\end{equation}
  1566→Evaluating \eqref{eq:casimir_roots} at $s = 1/2$ ($C_2 = 3/4$)
  1567→and $s = 1$ ($C_2 = 2$):
  1568→\begin{align}
  1569→x_+(1/2) &= \tfrac{1}{8}\bigl(-3 + \sqrt{57}\bigr)
  1570→  \,,\quad\text{so } x_+(1/2)\,m^2 = M_W^2\,,
  1571→\\[4pt]
  1572→x_+(1) &= -1 + \sqrt{3}
  1573→  \,,\quad\text{so } x_+(1)\,m^2 = M_Z^2\,.
  1574→\end{align}
  1575→The identification with $W$ and $Z$ is motivated by their
  1576→$SU(2)$ quantum numbers ($s = 1/2$ maps to the doublet structure of $W$,
  1577→$s = 1$ to the triplet).  Then
  1578→\begin{equation}
  1579→R = 1 - \frac{x_+(1/2)}{x_+(1)}
  1580→  = \frac{(\sqrt{19} - 3)(\sqrt{19} - \sqrt{3}\,)}{16}
  1581→  \;\approx\; 0.2231013\ldots
  1582→\end{equation}
  1583→The factored form follows from the discriminants of
  1584→\eqref{eq:casimir_quartic}: $57 = 3 \times 19$ at $s = 1/2$ and
  1585→$12 = 4 \times 3$ at $s = 1$.  Rationalizing the denominator
  1586→$\sqrt{3} - 1$ yields an expression in $\sqrt{19}$ and $\sqrt{3}$
  1587→alone, which coincides term-by-term with the form found
  1588→empirically by de~Vries.
  1589→
  1590→This should be compared with the on-shell electroweak mixing
  1591→parameter:
  1592→\begin{equation}
  1593→\label{eq:sin2_onshell}
  1594→\sin^2\!\theta_W^{\text{os}}
  1595→  \;\equiv\; 1 - \frac{M_W^2}{M_Z^2}
  1596→  = 0.22320 \pm 0.00026\,,
  1597→\end{equation}
  1598→using $M_Z = 91.1876 \pm 0.0021\GeV$ and
  1599→$M_W = 80.369 \pm 0.013\GeV$ (PDG~2024).
  1600→
  1601→Since $R = 1 - x_+(1/2)/x_+(1)$ and
  1602→$\sin^2\!\theta_W = 1 - M_W^2/M_Z^2$, the two expressions are
  1603→\emph{algebraically identical} once one identifies
  1604→$x_+(s) = M^2(s)/m^2$.  The prediction $R \approx
  1605→\sin^2\!\theta_W$ and the $W$-mass prediction derived below are
  1606→therefore \textbf{one and the same observation}, not two
  1607→independent tests.
  1608→
  1609→\paragraph{$W$-mass prediction.}
  1610→From $M_W = M_Z\sqrt{1 - R}$:
  1611→\begin{equation}
  1612→M_W^{\text{pred}}
  1613→  = 91.1876 \times \sqrt{1 - 0.22310}
  1614→  = 80.374 \pm 0.002\GeV\,,
  1615→\end{equation}
  1616→which lies $0.39\sigma$ from the PDG average
  1617→$M_W = 80.369 \pm 0.013\GeV$ and strongly disfavors the
  1618→anomalous CDF-II measurement $M_W^{\text{CDF}} = 80.4335 \pm
  1619→0.0094\GeV$ (6.2$\sigma$ tension with $R$).
  1620→
  1621→\paragraph{Scale parameter.}
  1622→Setting $x_+(1)\,m^2 = M_Z^2$ gives
  1623→\begin{equation}
  1624→m = \frac{M_Z}{\sqrt{\sqrt{3} - 1}}
  1625→  = 106.577\GeV
  1626→  \approx m_\mu \times 10^3\,,
  1627→\end{equation}
  1628→a coincidence whose significance is unclear.
  1629→
  1630→\subsection{Open issues for the Casimir equation}
  1631→\label{subsec:casimir_open}
  1632→
  1633→\paragraph{Renormalization scheme dependence.}
  1634→The comparison \eqref{eq:sin2_onshell} uses the on-shell
  1635→definition $\sin^2\!\theta_W = 1 - M_W^2/M_Z^2 = 0.22320$,
  1636→\textbf{not} the $\overline{\text{MS}}$ value
  1637→$\sin^2\!\theta_W(\overline{\text{MS}}) = 0.23122 \pm 0.00004$.
  1638→The two differ by $\Delta \approx 0.008$, far larger than
  1639→experimental errors.  The algebraic argument
  1640→\eqref{eq:casimir_quartic} contains no dynamical mechanism to
  1641→prefer one scheme over the other.  Until such a mechanism is
  1642→identified, the on-shell agreement should be regarded as
  1643→suggestive but scheme-dependent.
  1644→
  1645→\paragraph{The fourth eigenvalue.}
  1646→At $s = 1/2$, the second root of \eqref{eq:casimir_quartic} gives
  1647→\begin{equation}
  1648→|M(1/2, -)|
  1649→  = m\,\sqrt{|x_-(1/2)|}
  1650→  = 122.4\GeV\,,
  1651→\end{equation}
  1652→which is 2.3\% below $M_H = 125.25 \pm 0.17\GeV$.  However,
  1653→this eigenstate carries spin $1/2$, while the Higgs boson is
  1654→spin $0$.  The near-degeneracy is noted as a numerical curiosity;
  1655→we do not claim a connection.
  1656→
  1657→\paragraph{Origin.}
  1658→This observation was first made by H.~de~Vries in a Physics
  1659→Forums discussion (November 2004) and subsequently studied in
  1660→\cite{Rivero2005, Rivero2005b}.
  1661→
  1662→%----------------------------------------------------------------------
  1663→\subsection{Independence of the two structures}
  1664→\label{subsec:two_gap}
  1665→%----------------------------------------------------------------------
  1666→
  1667→Parts~A and~B describe independent algebraic structures that share
  1668→the same physical particles:
  1669→\begin{itemize}
  1670→\item The $SO(32)$ embedding (\S\ref{subsec:so32_chain}) governs
  1671→  the \emph{representation content}---which $SU(5)$ multiplets
  1672→  appear.
  1673→\item The Casimir equation (\S\ref{subsec:casimir_eq}) governs
  1674→  the \emph{mass/coupling pattern}---what numerical ratios emerge
  1675→  among the gauge-boson masses.
  1676→\item The two structures live over different number fields:
  1677→  the adjoint decomposition involves only rational
  1678→  dimensions, while the eigenvalue ratio $R$ lies in
  1679→  $\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt{3},\sqrt{19}\,)$---an extension not
  1680→  generated by any invariant of $SO(32)$.
  1681→\end{itemize}
  1682→No algebraic homomorphism linking the embedding chain
  1683→\eqref{eq:so32_chain} to the eigenvalue equation
  1684→\eqref{eq:casimir_quartic} has been found.  The gap between
  1685→\emph{content} (which states exist) and \emph{dynamics}
  1686→(what masses they acquire) remains the central open problem of
  1687→the sBootstrap program.
  1688→
  1689→%======================================================================
  1690→\section{SUSY Breaking: From Seeds to Spectrum}
  1691→\label{sec:breaking}
  1692→%======================================================================
  1693→
  1694→The SUSY-breaking story begins with the foundational observation 
  1695→\eqref{eq:pimu}: $m_\pi^2 - m_\mu^2 \approx f_\pi^2$. This is the 
  1696→Volkov--Akulov mass formula for a pseudo-Goldstino---the muon---whose 
  1697→superpartner---the pion---is a pseudo-Goldstone boson. The SUSY-breaking 
  1698→scale is $f_\pi \approx 92\MeV$, and the breaking mechanism is chiral: 
  1699→$\qqbar \neq 0$ breaks both chiral symmetry and the sBootstrap SUSY 
  1700→simultaneously. The Volkov--Akulov realization is the \emph{physical 
  1701→starting point}; what follows in this section is the SQCD machinery 
  1702→that makes it precise.
  1703→
  1704→The dynamics must accomplish three things: generate the seed-to-bloom 
  1705→transition for each tuple, preserve the energy-balance condition 
  1706→$\langle z_k^2\rangle = z_0^2$ through the breaking, and produce 
  1707→the correct splitting between meson and lepton masses at scale $f_\pi$.
  1708→
  1709→\subsection{The Seiberg framework}
  1710→
  1711→The natural arena is $\mathcal{N}=1$ SQCD with gauge group $SU(3)_c$ 
  1712→and $N_f$ flavors of chiral superfields $Q^i$, $\bar{Q}_j$ in the 
  1713→fundamental and antifundamental representations. For $N_f = N_c = 3$ 
  1714→(the case relevant to light QCD), Seiberg's exact results 
  1715→\cite{Seiberg1995} establish that the theory confines, with the 
  1716→low-energy degrees of freedom being the composite meson superfield
  1717→\begin{equation}
  1718→M^i{}_j = Q^i \cdot \bar{Q}_j\,,
  1719→\end{equation}
  1720→a $3\times 3$ matrix of chiral superfields, together with baryons 
  1721→$B = \epsilon_{ijk}Q^iQ^jQ^k$ and $\bar{B}$. These satisfy the 
  1722→quantum-modified constraint
  1723→\begin{equation}
  1724→\label{eq:seiberg}
  1725→\det M - B\bar{B} = \Lambda^{6}\,,
  1726→\end{equation}
  1727→where $\Lambda$ is the dynamical scale ($2N_c = 6$ for $N_c = 3$).
  1728→
  1729→Each entry $M^i{}_j$ is a chiral superfield containing a complex 
  1730→scalar (the meson) and a Weyl fermion (the lepton, in the sBootstrap 
  1731→identification). The scalar and fermion are degenerate in the SUSY 
  1732→limit; SUSY breaking splits them.
  1733→
  1734→\subsection{The three chiral superfields of each seed}
  1735→
  1736→The seed triple $(0, \zch_a, \zch_b)$ maps naturally onto three 
  1737→chiral superfields with the following mass structure before breaking:
  1738→
  1739→\begin{center}
  1740→\begin{tabular}{lcl}
  1741→\toprule
  1742→Superfield & Mass & Role \\
  1743→\midrule
  1744→$\Phi_0$ & $m_0 = 0$ & Massless: flat direction \\
  1745→$\Phi_a$ & $m_a = \zch_a^2$ & Light massive member \\
  1746→$\Phi_b$ & $m_b = \zch_b^2$ & Heavy massive member \\
  1747→\bottomrule
  1748→\end{tabular}
  1749→\end{center}
  1750→
  1751→The ratio $\zch_a/\zch_b = 2 - \sqrt{3}$ is enforced by the 
  1752→Koide energy-balance condition applied to the seed.
  1753→
  1754→In the Seiberg description, these three superfields are entries of 
  1755→the meson matrix $M^i{}_j$. For the $(0, s, c)$ seed, the relevant 
  1756→entries involve the strange and charm quarks; $\Phi_0$ is the entry 
  1757→whose quark-mass combination vanishes in the seed limit (i.e., the 
  1758→direction in flavor space where $m_i + m_j \to 0$ through GOR).
  1759→
  1760→\subsection{F-term breaking: the O'Raifeartaigh mechanism}
  1761→\label{subsec:fterm}
  1762→
  1763→The VA relation \eqref{eq:pimu} tells us \emph{that} SUSY is broken at 
  1764→scale $f_\pi$. To understand \emph{how}, we need the microscopic 
  1765→mechanism. The O'Raifeartaigh model \cite{ORaifeartaigh} is the simplest 
  1766→realization of spontaneous $F$-term SUSY breaking. With three chiral 
  1767→superfields, the superpotential
  1768→\begin{equation}
  1769→W = f\,\Phi_0 + m\,\Phi_1\Phi_2 + g\,\Phi_0\Phi_1^2
  1770→\end{equation}
  1771→gives $F$-terms:
  1772→\begin{align}
  1773→F_{\Phi_0}^* &= f + g\,\phi_1^2\,, \\
  1774→F_{\Phi_1}^* &= m\,\phi_2 + 2g\,\phi_0\phi_1\,, \\
  1775→F_{\Phi_2}^* &= m\,\phi_1\,.
  1776→\end{align}
  1777→If $f \neq 0$ and $m \neq 0$, then $F_{\Phi_2}^* = 0$ requires 
  1778→$\phi_1 = 0$, but then $F_{\Phi_0}^* = f \neq 0$: SUSY is 
  1779→\textbf{necessarily broken}. The key features are:
  1780→\begin{enumerate}
  1781→\item A massless field ($\Phi_0$) exists at tree level---it is the 
  1782→pseudo-modulus.
  1783→\item The breaking is triggered because $F_{\Phi_0}$ cannot vanish.
  1784→\item After including loop corrections, $\Phi_0$ acquires a mass 
  1785→(the Coleman--Weinberg potential lifts the flat direction).
  1786→\end{enumerate}
  1787→
  1788→The structural parallel with the seed triples is:
  1789→
  1790→\begin{center}
  1791→\begin{tabular}{llll}
  1792→\toprule
  1793→O'Raifeartaigh & Flavor & Seed & After breaking \\
  1794→\midrule
  1795→$\Phi_0$ (massless) & $b$ / $B$ & $\zch = 0$ (flat direction) & $\zch_b = 64.65$ (mass generated) \\
  1796→$\Phi_1$ (light) & $s$ / $\pi$ & $\zch_s = 9.67$ & $\zch \to -9.67$ (sign-flipped) \\
  1797→$\Phi_2$ (heavy) & $c$ / $D_s$ & $\zch_c = 35.64$ & $\zch_c \approx 35.64$ (shifted) \\
  1798→\midrule
  1799→ & & $f = 0$ (SUSY) & $f = f_\pi$ (broken) \\
  1800→\bottomrule
  1801→\end{tabular}
  1802→\end{center}
  1803→
  1804→This parallel is more than structural: the minimal O'Raifeartaigh
  1805→model gives $\Phi_1$ and $\Phi_2$ a single mass parameter $m$, but the
  1806→\emph{fermionic} mass matrix at the vacuum ($\phi_1 = 0$,
  1807→$\phi_0 = v$) is
  1808→\begin{equation*}
  1809→  W_{ij} = \begin{pmatrix} 0 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 2gv & m \\ 0 & m & 0 \end{pmatrix},
  1810→\end{equation*}
  1811→with eigenvalues $0$ (the goldstino) and $m_\pm = \sqrt{g^2v^2 + m^2} \pm gv$.
  1812→The mass ratio $m_-/m_+ = (\sqrt{t^2+1} - t)^2$, where $t = gv/m$,
  1813→is \emph{not} unity.  Setting $\sqrt{m_-}/\sqrt{m_+} = 2 - \sqrt{3}$
  1814→(the Koide seed ratio) requires $\sqrt{t^2+1} - t = 2 - \sqrt{3}$,
  1815→which gives
  1816→\begin{equation*}
  1817→  t = \frac{gv}{m} = \sqrt{3}\,,
  1818→\end{equation*}
  1819→the unique value at which $t^2 + 1 = 4$, so $m_\pm = (2 \pm \sqrt{3})\,m$.
  1820→The Koide ratio for the triple $(0, m_-, m_+) = (0,\, (2-\sqrt{3})\,m,
  1821→\, (2+\sqrt{3})\,m)$ evaluates to $Q = 4m/(\sqrt{2-\sqrt{3}} +
  1822→\sqrt{2+\sqrt{3}})^2 = 4/6 = 2/3$ exactly, since
  1823→$\sqrt{2-\sqrt{3}}\cdot\sqrt{2+\sqrt{3}} = 1$ and the squared sum
  1824→telescopes to $6$.
  1825→
  1826→The Koide condition on the seed is therefore \emph{one algebraic
  1827→constraint on the superpotential parameters}: $gv = \sqrt{3}\,m$.
  1828→The coupling $g$, the pseudo-modulus VEV $v$, and the mass
  1829→parameter $m$ are individually undetermined, but their ratio is fixed.
  1830→This is the microscopic content of the energy-balance condition
  1831→$\langle z_k^2\rangle = z_0^2$ applied to the seed.
  1832→
  1833→The $b$ quark and $B$ meson are
  1834→always present as flavor degrees of freedom; the seed configuration
  1835→$(0, s, c)$ means the $b$-direction sits at the flat direction before
  1836→breaking. The bloom is not particle creation but mass generation:
  1837→breaking lifts $\Phi_0$ from zero to $\zch_b \neq 0$, simultaneously
  1838→flipping the sign of $\zch_s$, so that the same three flavors
  1839→rearrange from $(0, s, c)$ into $(-s, c, b)$.
  1840→
  1841→\paragraph{The bloom as a phase rotation.}
  1842→In the Koide parametrization~\eqref{eq:koide_angle}, the seed sits at
  1843→$\delta = 3\pi/4$, where $\cos(3\pi/4) = -1/\sqrt{2}$ forces one
  1844→mass to vanish: $\sqrt{m_0} = \sqrt{M_0}(1 + \sqrt{2}\cos\delta) = 0$.
  1845→A Koide-preserving bloom ($Q = 2/3$ maintained) is a pure rotation
  1846→$\delta: 3\pi/4 \to \delta_{\text{phys}}$ at fixed scale $v_0$
  1847→and fixed amplitude $r = \sqrt{2}\,v_0$. The total mass
  1848→$M = 6v_0^2$ is conserved---mass merely redistributes among the three
  1849→eigenvalues. The sign flip is instantaneous: the seed sits exactly at
  1850→the boundary $\zch_0 = 0$, and any infinitesimal rotation generates
  1851→a nonzero charge with definite sign.
  1852→
  1853→Explicit R-symmetry breaking in the O'Raifeartaigh model (adding a
  1854→term $\delta W \sim \varepsilon\,\Phi_0\Phi_2$) lifts the goldstino
  1855→mass as $m_0 \sim \varepsilon^2/m$, but simultaneously pushes
  1856→$Q$ away from $2/3$---no nonzero $\varepsilon$ preserves the Koide
  1857→condition. The Koide seed is an \emph{unstable fixed point} of the
  1858→R-breaking perturbation. In the dynamical (SQCD) realization, the
  1859→bloom must therefore be a nonperturbative rearrangement---not a
  1860→smooth deformation of the O'Raifeartaigh spectrum.
  1861→
  1862→A striking difference from standard O'Raifeartaigh: the 
  1863→pseudo-modulus $\Phi_0$ normally acquires the \emph{smallest} mass 
  1864→in the spectrum (a loop-suppressed Coleman--Weinberg mass, 
  1865→parametrically $\sim g^2 f/16\pi^2 m$). In the sBootstrap bloom, 
  1866→the $b$-direction acquires the \emph{largest} mass 
  1867→($m_b \gg m_s$ and $m_b > m_c$). The bloom is far more dramatic than a 
  1868→perturbative CW lifting---it is a nonperturbative, strong-dynamics 
  1869→effect in which confinement generates a mass of order $\Lambda_{\text{QCD}}^2/m_q$, 
  1870→not $g^2/(16\pi^2)$. Numerically, the one-loop CW mass for the
  1871→pseudo-modulus at the Seiberg vacuum is $m_{\text{CW}} \approx 13\MeV$,
  1872→more than two orders of magnitude below the physical $m_b = 4{,}180\MeV$.
  1873→This inversion is a signature of the dynamical (SQCD) realization
  1874→versus the perturbative (superpotential) one.
  1875→
  1876→\paragraph{A candidate mechanism: fractional instantons.}
  1877→On $\mathbb{R}^3 \times S^1$, SU($N_c$) instantons fractionalize into
  1878→$N_c$ monopole-instantons \cite{Unsal2008}. Each monopole-instanton
  1879→carries a single fermionic zero mode per flavor; when the zero mode is
  1880→lifted by the quark mass $m_i$, the amplitude acquires a factor
  1881→$\sqrt{m_i}$ (not $m_i$). Since the $N_c = 3$ monopole contributions
  1882→enter \emph{additively} in the effective superpotential,
  1883→\begin{equation*}
  1884→  W_{\text{mon}} \sim \zeta\,\bigl(\sqrt{m_1} + \sqrt{m_2} + \sqrt{m_3}\bigr)\,,
  1885→\end{equation*}
  1886→where $\zeta \propto e^{-S_0/3}$ is the monopole fugacity. This is
  1887→\emph{exactly} the sum-of-square-roots structure of the vacuum charge
  1888→$v_0 = (\sum \sqrt{m_k})/3$.
  1889→The $v_0$-doubling then maps to the counting of active
  1890→monopole-instanton contributions: the seed (two active flavors) involves
  1891→two monopole amplitudes, while the bloom (three active flavors, one with
  1892→a sign flip) involves three, doubling $v_0$.
  1893→
  1894→This mechanism is non-holomorphic in the sense required: the
  1895→K\"ahler potential receives monopole--anti-monopole (bion) cross terms
  1896→of the form $|\!\sum s_k\sqrt{m_k}|^2$, where $m_k$ are the
  1897→current quark masses. Expanding for $N_c = 3$:
  1898→\begin{equation*}
  1899→  K_{\text{bion}} = \frac{\zeta^2}{\Lambda^2}\bigl(
  1900→  m_1 + m_2 + m_3
  1901→  + 2s_1 s_2\sqrt{m_1 m_2}
  1902→  + 2s_1 s_3\sqrt{m_1 m_3}
  1903→  + 2s_2 s_3\sqrt{m_2 m_3}\bigr)\,,
  1904→\end{equation*}
  1905→where the diagonal terms $s_k^2 m_k = m_k$ are self-bions and the
  1906→cross terms $\sqrt{m_i m_j}$ are mixed bion amplitudes---their
  1907→geometric mean structure is what produces $\sum\sqrt{m_k}$.
  1908→Note that $m_k$ here are the quark masses (superpotential couplings),
  1909→\emph{not} the meson VEVs $M^k{}_k \propto 1/m_k$ of the Seiberg
  1910→seesaw; the bion amplitude involves quark zero modes, not meson fields.
  1911→Organizing the bion amplitudes into
  1912→seed-sector ($S_{\text{seed}} = \sqrt{m_s} + \sqrt{m_c}$) and
  1913→bloom-sector ($S_{\text{bloom}} = -\!\sqrt{m_s} + \sqrt{m_c} + \sqrt{m_b}$)
  1914→channels, the K\"ahler correction takes the form
  1915→\begin{equation*}
  1916→  \delta K \sim \lambda_1\,|S_{\text{seed}}|^2
  1917→  + \lambda_2\,|S_{\text{bloom}} - 2\,S_{\text{seed}}|^2\,.
  1918→\end{equation*}
  1919→The second term is a perfect square that vanishes
  1920→\emph{at the minimum} when $S_{\text{bloom}} = 2\,S_{\text{seed}}$,
  1921→i.e., $\sqrt{m_b} = 3\sqrt{m_s} + \sqrt{m_c}$---the
  1922→$v_0$-doubling condition. The relation between bion couplings
  1923→($\lambda_3 = -4\lambda_2$ for cross-channel bions) is what
  1924→enforces the doubling. Numerically,
  1925→$S_{\text{bloom}} - 2\,S_{\text{seed}} = 0.023\MeV^{1/2}$
  1926→(effectively zero: $m_b = 4{,}177$ vs.\ PDG $4{,}180$).
  1927→The Hessian of $\lambda_2\,|S_{\text{bloom}} - 2\,S_{\text{seed}}|^2$
  1928→has rank~1: its single nonzero eigenvector is
  1929→$\partial f/\partial m_i$ (the gradient of
  1930→$f = \sqrt{m_b} - 3\sqrt{m_s} - \sqrt{m_c}$), while the two flat
  1931→directions correspond to independent variation of $m_s$ and $m_c$
  1932→at fixed $f$.  Thus the bion K\"ahler constrains exactly \emph{one}
  1933→mass combination; the remaining two masses are fixed by the Koide
  1934→seed in the superpotential. Including propagated uncertainties
  1935→($\delta m_s = 0.8\MeV$, $\delta m_c = 20\MeV$), the prediction
  1936→is $m_b = 4{,}177 \pm 40\MeV$, consistent with the PDG value
  1937→$4{,}180 \pm 30\MeV$ at $0.06\sigma$.
  1938→
  1939→The principal caveat is that this analysis
  1940→applies on $\mathbb{R}^3 \times S^1$; connecting to $\mathbb{R}^4$
  1941→physics requires \"Unsal's continuity conjecture~\cite{Unsal2012},
  1942→which posits that center-symmetric confinement at small $S^1$ is
  1943→continuously connected to the large-$S^1$ confining vacuum.
  1944→The conjecture is supported by lattice evidence for $SU(N)$ pure
  1945→Yang--Mills and by the absence of phase transitions in the
  1946→center-stabilized theory, but remains unproven for theories with
  1947→matter.
  1948→
  1949→The identification $\langle F_{\Phi_0}\rangle = f \sim \fpi^2$ is the
  1950→sBootstrap's central claim: the SUSY-breaking $F$-term is the chiral
  1951→condensate parameter.
  1952→
  1953→In SQCD, this is realized dynamically rather than through a 
  1954→superpotential: the chiral condensate $\qqbar \neq 0$ acts as the 
  1955→nonzero $F$-term, the impossibility of restoring chiral symmetry 
  1956→in the QCD vacuum is the dynamical analogue of $f \neq 0$, and the 
  1957→pion---the would-be Goldstone boson that acquires mass from explicit 
  1958→chiral breaking---plays the role of $\Phi_0$ whose mass is lifted 
  1959→by loop corrections (here, by quark masses).
  1960→
  1961→\subsection{The Goldstino direction and the Volkov--Akulov realization}
  1962→\label{subsec:va}
  1963→
  1964→When SUSY is spontaneously broken, a massless fermion appears: the 
  1965→Goldstino, the fermionic analogue of the Goldstone boson. Its decay 
  1966→constant $f_{\text{VA}}$ sets the SUSY-breaking scale through the 
  1967→Volkov--Akulov Lagrangian \cite{VolkovAkulov1973}:
  1968→\begin{equation}
  1969→\mathcal{L}_{\text{VA}} = -f_{\text{VA}}^2 \det\!\left(
  1970→\delta_\mu^\nu + \frac{i}{f_{\text{VA}}^2}\,\bar{\lambda}\bar{\sigma}^\nu
  1971→\partial_\mu\lambda\right),
  1972→\end{equation}
  1973→where $\lambda$ is the Goldstino field. At low energies, the Goldstino 
  1974→couples derivatively with strength $\sim 1/f_{\text{VA}}^2$, exactly 
  1975→as the pion couples with strength $\sim 1/f_\pi^2$ in the chiral 
  1976→Lagrangian.
  1977→
  1978→The sBootstrap identification is:
  1979→\begin{equation}
  1980→f_{\text{VA}} = f_\pi\,, \qquad 
  1981→\text{Goldstino} = \text{muon (partner of }\pi\text{)}\,.
  1982→\end{equation}
  1983→More precisely, the muon is a \emph{pseudo-Goldstino}: in the 
  1984→limit $m_q \to 0$ where the pion becomes exactly massless (a true 
  1985→Goldstone boson), its fermionic partner also becomes massless (a 
  1986→true Goldstino). The finite quark masses explicitly break both 
  1987→chiral symmetry (giving $m_\pi \neq 0$) and the sBootstrap SUSY 
  1988→(giving $m_\mu \neq m_\pi$), with the splitting controlled by 
  1989→$f_\pi$:
  1990→\begin{equation}
  1991→m_\pi^2 - m_\mu^2 = f_\pi^2\,.
  1992→\end{equation}
  1993→
  1994→This is the Volkov--Akulov relation for the pseudo-Goldstino: the 
  1995→mass splitting between the scalar (pion) and its fermionic partner 
  1996→(muon) is set by the SUSY-breaking scale squared.
  1997→
  1998→The VA mechanism is not a separate breaking from the F-term: it is 
  1999→the \emph{low-energy, composite-level description} of the same

2000→breaking. At the microscopic level, $\langle F\rangle \neq 0$ 
  2001→in the meson superfield; at the composite level, this manifests 
  2002→as the Goldstino coupling with $f_{\text{VA}} = f_\pi$. These 
  2003→two quantities---the Goldstino decay constant and the pion decay 
  2004→constant---are \emph{identified} in the sBootstrap; this is the 
  2005→central claim, not a derivation.
  2006→
  2007→The identification should not be confused with the standard relation
  2008→between the $F$-term VEV and the chiral condensate. In standard
  2009→SQCD,
  2010→%
  2011→\begin{equation*}
  2012→\langle F\rangle \sim \frac{|\qqbar|}{\fpi} \sim
  2013→\frac{(250\MeV)^3}{92\MeV} \approx 1.7\times 10^5\MeV^2\,,
  2014→\end{equation*}
  2015→%
  2016→while $\fpi^2 \approx 8{,}500\MeV^2$---a factor of ${\sim}20$
  2017→discrepancy. The GOR relation \cite{GOR1968},
  2018→$m_\pi^2 \fpi^2 = -(m_u + m_d)\qqbar$, connects these through the
  2019→small quark masses: the ratio $|\qqbar|/\fpi \sim 20$ is exactly
  2020→$(m_u + m_d)^{-1} m_\pi^2 \fpi \approx 20$, so the discrepancy is
  2021→the chiral suppression factor, not a tuning. The sBootstrap
  2022→identification $f_{\text{VA}} = f_\pi$ is therefore a
  2023→\emph{low-energy effective} statement: the breaking scale appearing
  2024→in the meson--lepton mass relation is $f_\pi$ (the Goldstone decay
  2025→constant), not the microscopic condensate $|\qqbar|^{1/3}$.
  2026→
  2027→The VA realization gives more than a mass formula---it organizes the 
  2028→entire breaking narrative. In the chiral limit ($m_q \to 0$), both 
  2029→pion and muon are exactly massless: the pion as the Goldstone boson 
  2030→of broken chiral symmetry, the muon as the Goldstino of broken 
  2031→sBootstrap SUSY. This is the \emph{exact seed}: the $B$-meson 
  2032→direction sits at the flat direction ($\zch = 0$), and the 
  2033→$\pi$--$\mu$ pair is massless. Turning on quark masses ($m_q \neq 0$) 
  2034→does three things simultaneously:
  2035→\begin{enumerate}
  2036→\item Gives the pion its GOR mass (lifting the flat direction for $\pi$).
  2037→\item Gives the muon its mass (the pseudo-Goldstino acquires mass 
  2038→when the explicit breaking parameter $m_q$ is nonzero).
  2039→\item Generates the $B$-meson mass (the flat direction for $\Phi_0$ 
  2040→is lifted by the Coleman--Weinberg potential, i.e., by the same 
  2041→quark masses that feed the GOR formula).
  2042→\end{enumerate}
  2043→The splitting between (1) and (2) is $f_\pi^2$---the VA relation. 
  2044→The seed-to-bloom transition of \S\ref{sec:two_tuples} is the same 
  2045→physics seen from the charge-space perspective: $m_q \neq 0$ lifts 
  2046→the zero, generates the $b$/$B$ mass, flips the sign of $\zch_s$ 
  2047→and $\zch_\pi$, and produces the full triple. The VA equation 
  2048→\eqref{eq:pimu} is not one fact among many; it is the generating 
  2049→relation from which the bloom follows.
  2050→
  2051→A note on quantum numbers: the Goldstino of spontaneously broken 
  2052→spacetime SUSY is a neutral fermion (the fermionic component of the 
  2053→superfield whose $F$-term breaks SUSY). The muon carries electric 
  2054→charge and lepton number. The sBootstrap SUSY, however, is not a 
  2055→spacetime symmetry but an emergent hadronic symmetry---a 
  2056→Miyazawa-type superalgebra \cite{Miyazawa} acting on composite 
  2057→states. In this context, the ``Goldstino'' need not carry the 
  2058→quantum numbers of a spacetime supercharge; it carries the quantum 
  2059→numbers appropriate to the broken hadronic generator. The muon's 
  2060→charge is inherited from the pion multiplet it partners.
  2061→
  2062→\subsection{D-term breaking: electromagnetic splittings}
  2063→The unbroken $U(1)_{\text{em}}$ gauge symmetry allows a 
  2064→Fayet--Iliopoulos (FI) term \cite{FayetIliopoulos}:
  2065→\begin{equation}
  2066→\mathcal{L}_{\text{FI}} = \xi\,D_{\text{em}}\,,
  2067→\end{equation}
  2068→which shifts scalar masses by $\delta m^2 = Q_{\text{em}}\,\xi$, 
  2069→where $Q_{\text{em}}$ is the electric charge. This is the 
  2070→supersymmetric version of the electromagnetic mass difference.
  2071→
  2072→In QCD without SUSY, the electromagnetic mass splitting of the 
  2073→pion is given by the Das--Guralnik--Mathur--Low--Young (DGMLY) 
  2074→formula \cite{DGMLY1967}:
  2075→\begin{equation}
  2076→m_{\pi^\pm}^2 - m_{\pi^0}^2 = \frac{3\alpha_{\text{em}}}{4\pi}\,
  2077→m_\rho^2
  2078→\approx (35.5\MeV)^2\,,
  2079→\end{equation}
  2080→which in the SUSY framework becomes a D-term contribution:
  2081→\begin{equation}
  2082→\xi \sim \frac{3\alpha_{\text{em}}}{4\pi}\,m_\rho^2 
  2083→\sim (35\MeV)^2\,.
  2084→\end{equation}
  2085→
  2086→In charge space, the D-term shifts $\zch$ by an amount proportional 
  2087→to $Q_{\text{em}}$:
  2088→\begin{equation}
  2089→\zch \to \zch + \delta\zch\,, \qquad 
  2090→\delta\zch \approx \frac{Q_{\text{em}}\,\xi}{2\zch}\,.
  2091→\end{equation}
  2092→For the pion ($Q_{\text{em}} = \pm 1$, $\zch \approx 11.8$): 
  2093→$\delta\zch \approx 0.2\MeV^{1/2}$, matching the observed 
  2094→$\zch_{\pi^\pm} - \zch_{\pi^0} = 11.82 - 11.62 = 0.20\MeV^{1/2}$.
  2095→
  2096→The D-term effect is subleading to the F-term: 
  2097→$\xi \sim (35\MeV)^2$ versus $f_\pi^2 \sim (92\MeV)^2$. It 
  2098→operates \emph{within} each supermultiplet entry, splitting charged 
  2099→from neutral mesons, while the F-term splits bosons from fermions 
  2100→across the supermultiplet.
  2101→
  2102→Witten's inequality \cite{Witten1983} guarantees 
  2103→$m_{\pi^\pm} > m_{\pi^0}$ in QCD with electromagnetism---the 
  2104→charged pion is always heavier. In the SUSY framework this 
  2105→becomes the statement that the D-term contribution is positive 
  2106→for $Q_{\text{em}} \neq 0$, consistent with the FI sign.
  2107→
  2108→\subsection{Mapping the breaking to the tuples}
  2109→The tuple structure maps onto a layered breaking with two independent 
  2110→mechanisms (F-term and D-term) plus a separate heavy-sector origin. 
  2111→The Volkov--Akulov realization of \S\ref{subsec:va} is not a third 
  2112→mechanism: it is the low-energy, composite-level description of the 
  2113→same F-term breaking. The VA relation \eqref{eq:pimu} \emph{sets the 
  2114→scale} ($f_{\text{VA}} = f_\pi$); the O'Raifeartaigh/SQCD dynamics 
  2115→of \S\ref{subsec:fterm} \emph{provides the mechanism} 
  2116→($\langle F\rangle \neq 0$).
  2117→
  2118→\paragraph{Layer 1: F-term/VA (chiral condensate, scale $\sim f_\pi$).}
  2119→The chiral condensate $\qqbar \neq 0$ breaks both chiral symmetry 
  2120→and the sBootstrap SUSY. This is the primary breaking, affecting 
  2121→the light sector. The seed triples $(0, s, c)$ and $(0, \pi, D_s)$ 
  2122→are the pre-breaking configurations; the massless member is the flat 
  2123→direction whose $F$-term is forced nonzero by confinement. After 
  2124→breaking:
  2125→\begin{itemize}
  2126→\item The zero member is lifted and acquires negative charge 
  2127→($\zch < 0$), becoming the sign-flipped member of the full triple 
  2128→($-s$, $-\pi$).
  2129→\item The full triples $(-s,c,b)$ and $(-\pi, D_s, B)$ emerge, 
  2130→incorporating the $b$-quark and $B$-meson sectors.
  2131→\item The lepton triple $(e, \mu, \tau)$ is the fermionic shadow: 
  2132→the leptons acquire masses through the same condensate, with the 
  2133→muon as pseudo-Goldstino.
  2134→\end{itemize}
  2135→
  2136→\paragraph{Layer 2: D-term (electromagnetism, scale $\sim \alpha_{\text{em}} m_\rho$).}
  2137→The $U(1)_{\text{em}}$ FI term generates electromagnetic mass 
  2138→splittings within each multiplet. This is a perturbative correction 
  2139→on top of the F-term breaking, affecting only charged particles. 
  2140→It does not create new Koide triples but shifts existing ones by 
  2141→$O(\alpha_{\text{em}})$.
  2142→
  2143→\paragraph{Layer 3: The heavy sector (scale $\sim v_0^{(cbt)}$).}
  2144→The $(c,b,t)$ triple at $v_0^2 \approx 29.6\GeV$ operates far 
  2145→above the chiral scale. It has no seed (no massless member), no 
  2146→meson shadow (top does not hadronize), and its breaking may be 
  2147→connected to a different sector of the theory---possibly the 
  2148→electroweak Higgs mechanism.
  2149→
  2150→This layered structure involves three distinct scales, each with 
  2151→different physical meaning:
  2152→\begin{align}
  2153→\text{F-term (boson--fermion splitting):} &\quad 
  2154→f_\pi \approx 92\MeV\,, \quad f_\pi^2 \approx 8{,}500\MeV^2\,; \\
  2155→\text{D-term (EM fine structure):} &\quad 
  2156→\xi^{1/2} \approx 35\MeV\,, \quad \xi \approx 1{,}200\MeV^2\,; \\
  2157→\text{Heavy sector (Koide scale):} &\quad 
  2158→v_0^{(cbt)} \approx 172\MeV^{1/2}\,, \quad (v_0^{(cbt)})^2 \approx 29{,}600\MeV\,.
  2159→\end{align}
  2160→Comparing the squared scales: $f_\pi^2 \gg \xi$ (F-term dominates 
  2161→over D-term by a factor $\sim 7$, consistent with the D-term being 
  2162→a perturbative $O(\alpha_{\text{em}})$ correction). The heavy-sector 
  2163→scale $(v_0^{(cbt)})^2$ carries dimensions of $\MeV$ (not $\MeV^2$) 
  2164→because $v_0$ is a charge ($\MeV^{1/2}$), not an energy; direct 
  2165→comparison with $f_\pi$ requires specifying which physical quantity 
  2166→is being compared.
  2167→
  2168→\subsection{The O'Raifeartaigh structure in SQCD}
  2169→
  2170→In Seiberg's SQCD with $N_f = N_c = 3$, the quantum constraint 
  2171→\eqref{eq:seiberg} provides a natural setting for the 
  2172→O'Raifeartaigh mechanism. The constraint $\det M - B\bar{B} = \Lambda^6$ 
  2173→means the moduli space has a specific geometry: not all field directions 
  2174→are flat. When quark masses are added via a tree-level superpotential 
  2175→$W_{\text{tree}} = m_i M^i{}_i$ (sum over flavors), the $F$-terms become:
  2176→\begin{equation}
  2177→F_{M^i{}_j} = m_i\,\delta^i_j + \Lambda^6\,(\text{constraint terms})\,.
  2178→\end{equation}
  2179→
  2180→For the seed triple $(0, s, c)$, the ``$0$'' member corresponds to a 
  2181→direction in the meson matrix where the tree-level mass vanishes---the 
  2182→entry involving the lightest quark combination. In the exact chiral 
  2183→limit ($m_u = m_d = 0$), this entry is truly massless, and the 
  2184→constraint \eqref{eq:seiberg} forces $\langle F\rangle \neq 0$ for 
  2185→that entry. This is the dynamical O'Raifeartaigh mechanism:
  2186→confinement (encoded in $\Lambda^6$) plays the role of the parameter
  2187→$f$ in the superpotential, and the impossibility of setting all
  2188→$F$-terms to zero is a consequence of the strong dynamics rather
  2189→than a choice of superpotential.
  2190→
  2191→\paragraph{The pseudo-modulus and the Koide seed.}
  2192→In the ISS magnetic dual, the tree-level superpotential contains a
  2193→Yukawa coupling $h\,\mathrm{Tr}(q\,M\,\tilde q)$ between the magnetic
  2194→quarks $q$, $\tilde q$ and the meson matrix $M$. The O'Raifeartaigh
  2195→parameters map as $g \to h$, $m \to h\mu$, $v \to \langle X\rangle$
  2196→(the pseudo-modulus VEV), so the seed Koide condition
  2197→$gv/m = \sqrt{3}$ becomes $\langle X\rangle/\mu = \sqrt{3}$---the
  2198→magnetic Yukawa~$h$ cancels. The Koide seed fixes the
  2199→pseudo-modulus VEV, not the coupling~$h$.
  2200→At one-loop Coleman--Weinberg level $\langle X\rangle = 0$; a
  2201→non-canonical K\"ahler correction provides the mechanism.
  2202→
  2203→\paragraph{K\"ahler stabilization of the pseudo-modulus.}
  2204→In the minimal O'Raifeartaigh model ($W = fX + m\phi\tilde\phi
  2205→+ gX\phi^2$) with canonical K\"ahler potential,
  2206→the CW potential is monotonically increasing and selects
  2207→$\langle X\rangle = 0$.  Polynomial superpotential deformations
  2208→(including cubic, quartic, and dimension-five operators)
  2209→either vanish at the vacuum, reinforce $v = 0$, or produce
  2210→minima at $gv/m \ll \sqrt{3}$.
  2211→
  2212→A negative quartic K\"ahler correction resolves this:
  2213→\begin{equation*}
  2214→  K = |X|^2 + \frac{c}{\Lambda_K^2}\,|X|^4\,,
  2215→  \qquad c < 0\,.
  2216→\end{equation*}
  2217→The tree-level potential becomes
  2218→$V_{\text{tree}} = f^2/(1 + 4c\,v^2/\Lambda_K^2)$, which has
  2219→a pole at $v_{\text{pole}} = \Lambda_K/(2\sqrt{|c|})$.
  2220→Near the pole the CW contribution is negligible
  2221→($V_{\text{CW}} \sim 10^{-4}\,m^4 \ll V_{\text{tree}}$),
  2222→and the effective potential is minimized at $v_{\text{pole}}$.
  2223→Demanding $v_{\text{pole}} = \sqrt{3}\,m/g$ gives the exact condition
  2224→\begin{equation*}
  2225→  c = -\,\frac{\Lambda_K^2}{12\,m^2}\,.
  2226→\end{equation*}
  2227→With $\Lambda_K = m$ this is $c = -1/12$, an algebraic result:
  2228→$\Lambda_K/(2\sqrt{1/12}) = \sqrt{12}/2 = \sqrt{3}$.
  2229→No numerical tuning is required.
  2230→At this VEV the fermion spectrum is
  2231→$(0,\, (2{-}\sqrt{3})\,m,\, (2{+}\sqrt{3})\,m)$
  2232→with $Q = 4/6 = 2/3$ exactly
  2233→(since $(\sqrt{2{-}\sqrt{3}} + \sqrt{2{+}\sqrt{3}})^2 = 4 + 2 = 6$),
  2234→confirming the Koide seed. The K\"ahler metric
  2235→$K_{X\bar X} = 1 - v^2/(3m^2)$ vanishes at $v = \sqrt{3}\,m$,
  2236→signaling the boundary of the effective field theory.
  2237→
  2238→The ISS mechanism (Intriligator--Seiberg--Shih \cite{ISS2006}) 
  2239→establishes metastable SUSY-breaking vacua for $N_c < N_f < 3N_c/2$, 
  2240→where the rank condition in the magnetic dual prevents all $F$-terms 
  2241→from vanishing. The sBootstrap case $N_f = N_c = 3$ is strictly 
  2242→\emph{below} the ISS window---at $N_f = N_c$ the low-energy 
  2243→description is qualitatively different, governed by the 
  2244→quantum-modified constraint \eqref{eq:seiberg} on the moduli space 
  2245→rather than by a free magnetic dual. However, physical QCD has
  2246→$N_f = 3$ light flavors and $N_f = 4$--$5$ if charm and bottom are
  2247→included at their respective thresholds. With charm included,
  2248→$N_f = 4$ falls within the ISS window ($3 < 4 < 4.5$); with both
  2249→charm and bottom, $N_f = 5$ falls in the conformal window
  2250→($4.5 < 5 < 9$), where the Seiberg seesaw constraint does not apply
  2251→directly. The sBootstrap's multi-scale structure---with
  2252→the seed triples involving $s$, $c$, and $b$ quarks at different
  2253→scales---requires treating the effective $N_f$ as
  2254→scale-dependent: $N_f = 3$ below $m_c$ (confining, Seiberg
  2255→constraint), $N_f = 4$ above $m_c$ (ISS metastable SUSY breaking),
  2256→and $N_f = 5$ above $m_b$ (conformal window).
  2257→Note that $N_f = 4$ is the \emph{unique} integer satisfying
  2258→$3 < N_f < 4.5$ for $N_c = 3$, providing a dynamical rationale for
  2259→exactly four light flavors $(u,d,s,c)$ in the SUSY-breaking sector.
  2260→
  2261→\paragraph{ISS vacuum lifetime.}
  2262→The metastable ISS vacuum decays to the SUSY-preserving ground state
  2263→by tunneling with bounce action
  2264→$S_{\text{bounce}} \sim (8\pi^2/3)\,N_c\,(\Lambda_{\text{mag}}/h\mu)^4$,
  2265→where $\mu$ is the lightest quark mass controlling the tunneling.
  2266→For $\mu = m_s = 93\MeV$, $h \sim O(1)$, and
  2267→$\Lambda_{\text{mag}} \sim 300\MeV$:
  2268→$S_{\text{bounce}} \sim 10^2$--$10^3$ (depending on~$h$),
  2269→giving a lifetime exceeding the age of the universe by hundreds of
  2270→orders of magnitude for any $h$ of order unity.
  2271→The metastable vacuum is cosmologically acceptable.
  2272→
  2273→\paragraph{The Seiberg seesaw and dual Koide.}
  2274→At the Seiberg vacuum with $B = \bar B = 0$, the diagonal meson VEVs
  2275→scale as $\langle M^j{}_j\rangle \propto 1/m_j$: heavy quarks produce
  2276→light mesons and vice versa. This seesaw prompts a dual question: does
  2277→the inverse-mass spectrum $\{1/m_i\}$ satisfy a Koide condition?
  2278→Evaluating $Q = \sum(1/m_i)/(\sum\sqrt{1/m_i})^2$ for the pure
  2279→down-type quarks $(d,s,b)$:
  2280→\begin{equation*}
  2281→  Q(1/m_d,\, 1/m_s,\, 1/m_b) = 0.6652\,,
  2282→\end{equation*}
  2283→a 0.22\% deviation from $2/3$---better than any direct quark Koide
  2284→triple. No other quark triple comes close under
  2285→the Seiberg seesaw (the next best, $(d,s,c)$, deviates by 4.2\%).
  2286→This ``dual Koide'' on $(d,s,b)$ (first reported here) connects
  2287→the UV mass hierarchy to the IR meson VEVs: the Seiberg constraint
  2288→maps the down-type quark masses into a near-Koide spectrum of
  2289→meson condensates. However, the
  2290→result is sensitive to $m_d$: within $\pm 1\sigma$ ($m_d = 4.67
  2291→\pm 0.48\MeV$), $Q$ ranges from $0.655$ to $0.676$, and the deviation
  2292→from $2/3$ varies from $0.2\%$ to $1.7\%$. The dual Koide is an
  2293→observation at the edge of the current $m_d$ uncertainty.
  2294→Setting $Q(1/m_d,1/m_s,1/m_b) = 2/3$ exactly predicts
  2295→$m_b = 4562\MeV$ ($9\%$ from PDG, $12.7\sigma$)---much worse
  2296→than $v_0$-doubling ($m_b = 4177\MeV$, $0.07\%$).  The $0.22\%$
  2297→closeness of $Q$ to $2/3$ does not translate to a precise mass
  2298→prediction because the Koide quadratic amplifies small $Q$
  2299→deviations into large mass deviations.
  2300→
  2301→A simple algebraic identity connects the seed to the seesaw:
  2302→for any two masses, the Koide ratio
  2303→$Q(a,b) = (a+b)/(\sqrt{a}+\sqrt{b})^2$ satisfies
  2304→$Q(a,b) = Q(1/a,\,1/b)$ identically.
  2305→The seed $(0, m_s, m_c)$ sits at this self-dual point:
  2306→its Koide ratio is the same whether evaluated on quark masses or
  2307→on Seiberg-dual meson VEVs $\propto 1/m_i$.  Thus the seed Koide
  2308→in the electric (UV) theory is \emph{automatically} a seed Koide
  2309→in the magnetic (IR) theory---the Seiberg duality preserves the
  2310→seed condition without fine-tuning.
  2311→For the full bloomed triple $(-s,c,b)$, self-duality breaks:
  2312→$Q(-s,c,b) \neq Q(1/m_s,1/m_c,1/m_b)$ in general.
  2313→The dual Koide $Q(1/m_d,1/m_s,1/m_b) = 0.665$ is therefore a
  2314→\emph{nontrivial} constraint on the down-type sector, not a
  2315→tautological restatement of the seed.
  2316→Among all $\binom{5}{3} = 10$ quark triples, $(d,s,b)$ is the
  2317→unique triple with inverse-mass Koide ratio within 1\% of $2/3$;
  2318→the next closest is $(d,s,c)$ at 4.2\% deviation---a 19:1 margin.
  2319→
  2320→\subsection{How the three supermultiplets join}
  2321→
  2322→The seed-to-bloom transition generates three separate Koide triples. 
  2323→But the full sBootstrap requires these triples to be \emph{embedded 
  2324→in a single meson matrix}. The $3\times 3$ light-flavor matrix is:
  2325→
  2326→\begin{equation}
  2327→M^{(3)} = \begin{pmatrix}
  2328→u\bar{u} & u\bar{d} & u\bar{s} \\
  2329→d\bar{u} & d\bar{d} & d\bar{s} \\
  2330→s\bar{u} & s\bar{d} & s\bar{s}
  2331→\end{pmatrix}
  2332→\;\longleftrightarrow\;
  2333→\begin{pmatrix}
  2334→\pi^0/\eta & \pi^+ & K^+ \\
  2335→\pi^- & \pi^0/\eta & K^0 \\
  2336→K^- & \bar{K}^0 & \eta'
  2337→\end{pmatrix}.
  2338→\end{equation}
  2339→
  2340→When charm and bottom are included, the matrix extends to $5\times 5$. 
  2341→We display it schematically, with the members of the meson Koide 
  2342→triple $(-\pi, D_s, B)$ marked by boxes:
  2343→
  2344→\begin{equation}
  2345→M^{(5)} = \begin{pmatrix}
  2346→\pi^0/\eta & \boxed{\pi^+} & K^+ & D^0 & \boxed{B^+} \\
  2347→\pi^- & \pi^0/\eta & K^0 & D^- & B^0 \\
  2348→K^- & \bar{K}^0 & \eta' & \boxed{D_s^-} & B_s \\
  2349→\bar{D}^0 & D^+ & D_s^+ & \eta_c & B_c \\
  2350→B^- & \bar{B}^0 & \bar{B}_s & \bar{B}_c & \eta_b
  2351→\end{pmatrix},
  2352→\end{equation}
  2353→where rows are indexed by $(u, d, s, c, b)$ and columns by 
  2354→$(\bar{u}, \bar{d}, \bar{s}, \bar{c}, \bar{b})$.
  2355→
  2356→The three members of the meson Koide triple occupy different 
  2357→off-diagonal blocks of this matrix: $\pi^+ = u\bar{d}$ is in the 
  2358→light $(u,d)$ block, $D_s^- = s\bar{c}$ bridges the light-charm 
  2359→sector, and $B^+ = u\bar{b}$ bridges the light-bottom sector. They 
  2360→do not form a ``slice'' in any simple linear-algebraic sense---they 
  2361→are scattered entries connected by the Koide condition on their 
  2362→\emph{masses}, not by their positions in the matrix. The meson seed 
  2363→$(0, \pi, D_s)$ drops the $B^+$ and replaces it with zero (the 
  2364→entry that would involve the top quark, absent from the matrix).
  2365→
  2366→This scattered structure is the central puzzle: the Koide triples 
  2367→are \emph{mass relations} among matrix entries, not submatrices. 
  2368→The question is whether the Seiberg constraint 
  2369→$\det M - B\bar{B} = \Lambda^6$ (appropriately generalized to 
  2370→$5 \times 5$), combined with the SUSY-breaking dynamics, produces 
  2371→correlations among these scattered entries that enforce the 
  2372→energy-balance condition $\langle z_k^2\rangle = z_0^2$.
  2373→
  2374→\subsection{The energy balance is not an RG attractor}
  2375→\label{subsec:fixedpoint}
  2376→One might hope that the energy-balance condition
  2377→$\langle z_k^2\rangle = z_0^2$ is a \emph{dynamical attractor}:
  2378→that three masses evolving under SUSY-breaking dynamics flow toward
  2379→the Koide surface $Q = 2/3$. However, the one-loop soft-mass RG
  2380→equations in $\mathcal{N}=1$ SQCD with universal Casimirs and
  2381→negligible Yukawas give $dm_i/dt = \gamma(M_g^2 - m_i)$, which
  2382→drives all masses to a common value $m_i \to M_g^2$, yielding
  2383→$Q \to 1/3$---the \emph{opposite} extreme. The Koide preservation
  2384→condition $\sum_i F_i(3 - 2S/\sqrt{m_i}) = 0$ on the $Q = 2/3$
  2385→surface is algebraically incompatible with any uniform attractor
  2386→flow (by the Cauchy--Schwarz inequality, the required identity
  2387→$S\sum 1/\sqrt{m_i} = 9/2$ contradicts $S\sum 1/\sqrt{m_i} \geq 9$).
  2388→
  2389→The energy-balance condition therefore does \emph{not} arise from
  2390→simple RG running. It must instead be a \emph{boundary condition}
  2391→on the superpotential---as in the O'Raifeartaigh realization of
  2392→\S\ref{subsec:fterm}, where $gv = \sqrt{3}\,m$ produces the exact
  2393→Koide seed. The condition is set at the scale of SUSY breaking and
  2394→then preserved by the QCD-invariant mass ratios, rather than being
  2395→an RG attractor.
  2396→
  2397→\subsection{The exact seed and what breaks it}
  2398→\label{subsec:exactseed}
  2399→
  2400→The seed configuration $(0, \zch_a, \zch_b)$ requires \emph{two} 
  2401→independent conditions:
  2402→\begin{enumerate}
  2403→\item One member at $\zch = 0$ exactly (the flat direction).
  2404→\item The ratio of the massive members equals $2 - \sqrt{3}$:
  2405→\begin{equation}
  2406→\frac{\sqrt{m_a}}{\sqrt{m_b}} = 2 - \sqrt{3} \approx 0.26795\,.
  2407→\end{equation}
  2408→\end{enumerate}
  2409→These are logically distinct. The chiral limit ($m_u = m_d = 0$, 
  2410→$\qqbar \neq 0$) enforces condition (1) for the meson sector: the 
  2411→pion is an exact Goldstone boson, $m_\pi = 0$, and in the 
  2412→picture of \S\ref{subsec:fterm} the $B$-meson direction sits at 
  2413→the flat direction before acquiring mass. But the chiral limit says 
  2414→\emph{nothing} about condition (2)---it does not constrain 
  2415→$\sqrt{m_{D_s}}/\sqrt{m_B}$ or $\sqrt{m_s}/\sqrt{m_c}$ 
  2416→to equal $2 - \sqrt{3}$.
  2417→
  2418→The physical deviations from the exact ratio are small but 
  2419→measurable:
  2420→
  2421→\begin{center}
  2422→\begin{tabular}{lccc}
  2423→\toprule
  2424→Seed & Exact ratio & Physical ratio & Deviation \\
  2425→\midrule
  2426→$(0, \pi, D_s)$ & 0.2680 & 
  2427→$\sqrt{m_\pi}/\sqrt{m_{D_s}} = 0.2663$ & $-0.6\%$ \\
  2428→$(0, s, c)$ & 0.2680 & 
  2429→$\sqrt{m_s}/\sqrt{m_c} = 0.2712$ & $+1.2\%$ \\
  2430→$(0 \approx e, \mu, \tau)$ & 0.2680 & 
  2431→$\sqrt{m_\mu}/\sqrt{m_\tau} = 0.2439$ & $-9.0\%$ \\
  2432→\bottomrule
  2433→\end{tabular}
  2434→\end{center}
  2435→
  2436→The meson seed is closest to the exact ratio (0.6\% deviation). 
  2437→The quark seed deviates by 1.2\%. A natural thought is that QCD 
  2438→running might shift the quark ratio: perhaps at some scale $\mu^*$ 
  2439→the ratio $\sqrt{m_s(\mu^*)}/\sqrt{m_c(\mu^*)}$ hits $2 - \sqrt{3}$ 
  2440→exactly. But this is \emph{not possible within QCD alone}. At leading 
  2441→order, all quark masses run with the same anomalous dimension 
  2442→$\gamma_m$:
  2443→\begin{equation}
  2444→m_i(\mu) = m_i(\mu_0)\left(\frac{\alpha_s(\mu)}{\alpha_s(\mu_0)}\right)^{\gamma_m/\beta_0},
  2445→\end{equation}
  2446→so the ratio $m_s(\mu)/m_c(\mu)$---and therefore 
  2447→$\sqrt{m_s}/\sqrt{m_c}$---is an RG invariant of QCD. Higher-order 
  2448→corrections break this invariance, but only at the $O(\alpha_s^2)$ 
  2449→level, far too small to account for 1.2\%.
  2450→
  2451→The quark seed deviation is therefore \emph{intrinsic}: the 
  2452→$\overline{\text{MS}}$ mass ratio $m_s/m_c$ is a fixed number 
  2453→(at any QCD scale), and it misses the Koide target by 1.2\%. 
  2454→Three possible explanations present themselves:
  2455→
  2456→\paragraph{Electroweak running.} Above the electroweak scale, 
  2457→quark masses are controlled by Yukawa couplings $y_i$ to the 
  2458→Higgs, which \emph{do} run differently for different flavors: 
  2459→the $\beta$-functions for $y_s$ and $y_c$ involve different 
  2460→electroweak quantum numbers ($SU(2)_L$ doublet structure, 
  2461→hypercharge). The ratio $y_s/y_c$ evolves with scale, and 
  2462→there may exist a scale $\mu^*$ above the electroweak 
  2463→threshold where $\sqrt{y_s(\mu^*)}/\sqrt{y_c(\mu^*)} = 2 - \sqrt{3}$. 
  2464→This would identify the ``seed scale'' as an electroweak-sector 
  2465→phenomenon, not a QCD one---consistent with the sBootstrap's 
  2466→identification of the breaking with $SU(2)_L \times U(1)_Y$ 
  2467→rather than $SU(3)_c$.
  2468→
  2469→\paragraph{Charge running.} Even if mass ratios are QCD-invariant,
  2470→the Koide decomposition $\zch_k = z_0 + z_k$ may have its own
  2471→flow. The Koide scale $v_0 = z_0^2$ sets the common scale,
  2472→and the fluctuations $z_k$ encode the deviations. As shown in
  2473→\S\ref{subsec:fixedpoint}, simple RG running drives $Q \to 1/3$
  2474→rather than $2/3$, so the energy-balance condition cannot be an
  2475→attractor in the usual sense. However, the O'Raifeartaigh
  2476→realization (\S\ref{subsec:fterm}) shows that the condition is a
  2477→boundary condition on the superpotential ($gv = \sqrt{3}\,m$).
  2478→The 1.2\% deviation of the quark seed ratio may then reflect
  2479→the distance between the physical quark masses and the exact
  2480→superpotential constraint.
  2481→
  2482→\paragraph{Meson vs.\ quark deviations.} The meson deviation 
  2483→(0.6\%) is smaller than the quark deviation (1.2\%), and for 
  2484→a different reason: meson masses are \emph{not} simple functions 
  2485→of quark mass ratios. The pion mass is given by the GOR 
  2486→relation $m_\pi^2 f_\pi^2 = -(m_u + m_d)\qqbar$; the $D_s$ 
  2487→mass involves heavy-quark effective theory; and both receive 
  2488→chiral perturbation theory corrections. These nonperturbative 
  2489→QCD effects are the dominant source of the meson seed deviation, 
  2490→and are in principle calculable at NLO in ChPT.
  2491→
  2492→\paragraph{A remarkable feature of the VA relation.} The 
  2493→foundational equation $m_\pi^2 - m_\mu^2 = f_\pi^2$ contains 
  2494→no QCD coupling constant: no $\alpha_s$, no $N_c$, no 
  2495→$\Lambda_{\text{QCD}}$. It is a pure mass relation among three 
  2496→experimentally measured quantities---two masses and a decay 
  2497→constant---holding to 2.2\%. In the sBootstrap, this is natural: 
  2498→the relation is a SUSY mass splitting, set by the breaking 
  2499→scale $f_\pi$, and SUSY mass relations do not involve the gauge

3000→\end{tabular}
  3001→\end{center}
  3002→The first four rows account for six quark masses ($m_u$ and $m_d$ free,
  3003→$m_s$ sets the scale, $m_c$ from seed, $m_b$ from K\"ahler, $m_t$
  3004→external). The meson--lepton splitting is universal across the
  3005→multiplet, and the $D$-term provides the subdominant electromagnetic
  3006→correction.
  3007→
  3008→The parameters of this construction separate into those fixed by
  3009→structure and those that remain free.
  3010→
  3011→\paragraph{Fixed by the Koide seed.}
  3012→The O'Raifeartaigh condition $gv/m = \sqrt{3}$ translates, in the
  3013→ISS magnetic dual, into $\langle X\rangle = \sqrt{3}\,\mu$ for the
  3014→pseudo-modulus.
  3015→This fixes the mass ratio $m_c/m_s = (2+\sqrt{3})^2$
  3016→within the seed triple, predicting $m_c = 1{,}301\MeV$ from $m_s$ alone
  3017→(PDG: $1{,}270\MeV$, 2.4\% deviation).
  3018→
  3019→\paragraph{Fixed by the vacuum-charge doubling.}
  3020→The bloom from seed to full triple doubles $v_0$, giving
  3021→$\sqrt{m_b} = 3\sqrt{m_s} + \sqrt{m_c}$ and predicting
  3022→$m_b = 4{,}177\MeV$ (0.1$\sigma$ from PDG). This constraint
  3023→is \emph{non-holomorphic}: it is a linear relation among square
  3024→roots of masses, while the superpotential is holomorphic in meson
  3025→fields whose VEVs scale as $1/m_i$. The map
  3026→$\zch_k = \sqrt{m_k} \sim 1/\!\sqrt{\langle M^k{}_k\rangle}$
  3027→is non-holomorphic. The $v_0$-doubling must therefore arise from the
  3028→K\"ahler potential, nonperturbative effects, or emergent dynamics---it
  3029→is not a tree-level superpotential term.
  3030→
  3031→\paragraph{Fixed by SUSY breaking.}
  3032→The supertrace relation $m^2_\pi - m^2_\mu = \fpi^2$ fixes the
  3033→splitting scale. At the Seiberg vacuum, $F_X = 0$ (SUSY is
  3034→unbroken in the confined sector), but the Yukawa couplings
  3035→produce $F_{H_u} = y_c M^c{}_c$ and $F_{H_d} = y_b M^b{}_b$.
  3036→By the seesaw $y_j M_j = (m_j/v)(C/m_j) = C/v$, these
  3037→F-terms are flavour-universal:
  3038→$F_{H_u} = F_{H_d} = C/v_{\text{EW}} = 290\MeV$.
  3039→The identification $\langle F\rangle \sim \fpi^2$
  3040→links $\Lambda_{\text{QCD}}$ to $m_s$ via the GOR relation.
  3041→
  3042→\paragraph{$D$-term effects on the Koide condition.}
  3043→In $\mathcal{N}=1$ SUSY, $D$-terms shift scalar masses but leave
  3044→fermion masses untouched. The lepton Koide triples, being conditions
  3045→on \emph{fermion} masses, are naturally protected against
  3046→$D$-term perturbations. The meson Koide triple
  3047→$(-\pi, D_s, B)$ involves scalar masses and \emph{is} in principle
  3048→affected by $D$-terms: a FI parameter $\xi$ shifts $m^2 \to m^2 + \xi Q_{\text{em}}^2$.
  3049→Since all three mesons carry unit charge, each $m^2$ shifts by the
  3050→same $\xi$, and the relative correction to $Q$ is of order
  3051→$\xi/(4m_\pi^2) \sim 0.1\%$ for $\xi \approx -80\MeV^2$.
  3052→Numerically, the $D$-term shifts the meson $Q$ by $0.03\%$,
  3053→well below the $0.10\%$ precision of the triple.
  3054→
  3055→\paragraph{Remaining free parameters.}
  3056→After the seed Koide fixes $m_c/m_s$, the $v_0$-doubling fixes
  3057→$m_b$, and $\langle X\rangle/\mu = \sqrt{3}$ is imposed, only two to three free
  3058→parameters remain:
  3059→\begin{itemize}
  3060→  \item $m_u$, $m_d$: unconstrained by any Koide triple; connected
  3061→    to the Cabibbo angle through the Gatto--Sartori--Tonin relation
  3062→    $|V_{us}| \approx \sqrt{m_d/m_s}$ (giving $V_{us} = 0.224$,
  3063→    PDG: $0.2243$);
  3064→  \item $m_s$ (or equivalently $\Lambda_{\text{QCD}}$): the overall
  3065→    scale of the light sector. The lepton Koide scale
  3066→    $M_0 \approx \fpi^2/27$ (\S\ref{sec:seeds}) ties the lepton
  3067→    scale to $\fpi$ and hence to $\Lambda_{\text{QCD}}$.
  3068→\end{itemize}
  3069→The top mass $m_t$ is external---it does not participate in the
  3070→confined meson matrix---but if $Q(c,b,t) = 2/3$ is imposed as a
  3071→constraint rather than treated as a test, $m_t$ is predicted from
  3072→$m_s$ alone. The Yukawa couplings
  3073→$y_c = \sqrt{2}\,m_c/v_{\text{EW}}$,
  3074→$y_b = \sqrt{2}\,m_b/v_{\text{EW}}$ are fixed by the predicted masses.
  3075→
  3076→The $(c,b,t)$ Koide triple provides an \emph{overdetermination test}.
  3077→Using the self-consistent predictions
  3078→$m_c^{\text{seed}} = 1{,}301\MeV$ and
  3079→$m_b^{v_0} = 4{,}233\MeV$ (from the seed and $v_0$-doubling
  3080→with input $m_s$ only), together with the external $m_t$:
  3081→$Q(c,b,t) = 0.668$, a $0.14\%$ deviation from~$2/3$---closer
  3082→than the $0.42\%$ obtained with PDG masses.
  3083→The self-consistent predictions satisfy the Koide conditions
  3084→better than the measured values.
  3085→If instead both overlapping triples are required to
  3086→satisfy $Q = 2/3$ exactly, the overlap system determines
  3087→$m_b^{\text{pred}} = 4{,}159\MeV$ (0.5\% from PDG) and
  3088→$m_c^{\text{pred}} = 1{,}369\MeV$ (7.8\% from PDG). The bion
  3089→K\"ahler prediction $m_b = 4{,}177\MeV$ ($0.07\%$, $0.06\sigma$)
  3090→is more precise, suggesting that $v_0$-doubling rather than exact
  3091→$Q = 2/3$ on both triples is the tighter constraint.
  3092→
  3093→Conversely, imposing $Q(c,b,t) = 2/3$ exactly on the self-consistent
  3094→$m_c^{\text{seed}}$ and $m_b^{v_0}$ predicts
  3095→$m_t = 171.3 \pm 1.5\GeV$ (where the uncertainty comes from
  3096→$\delta m_s = 0.8\MeV$), within $0.9\%$ of the PDG pole mass
  3097→$m_t = 172.76 \pm 0.30\GeV$.  The entire third-generation mass
  3098→spectrum then follows from a single input~$m_s$; using
  3099→$m_s = 94.2\MeV$ (within $1\sigma$ of PDG) gives $m_t$ exactly.
  3100→
  3101→\paragraph{Summary of predictions.}
  3102→Table~\ref{tab:predictions} collects the quantitative predictions.
